content.text: When a prompt template or pinned model version reaches end of life, the teams and systems that depend on it need time to migrate. Without a structured notification process, deprecations arrive as surprises — triggering emergency rewrites, production incidents, and broken SLAs. A well-run sunset notification process gives stakeholders enough lead time to migrate safely while holding a clear line on when the old version stops working.
What Constitutes a Prompt or Model Version
A versioned prompt template is a named, stable revision of a system prompt or few-shot prompt stored in version control or a prompt management system, referenced by version ID (e.g.,
). Any callers that pin to that version ID are affected by its sunset.
A pinned model ID is an explicit reference to a specific model version, such as
), pinned IDs lock behavior — and become subject to provider deprecation timelines.
Why a Structured Notification Process Matters
a prompt template may be consumed by multiple teams, API partners, and internal services — each with different migration timelines and technical constraints.
when a model provider removes a model version without adequate notice, callers that pin to the old ID receive errors. The 2023 GPT-4-0314 deprecation caught many teams unprepared.
migrating from one prompt version or model to another requires testing, evaluation, and sign-off. Teams need 30-90 days of lead time for non-trivial migrations.
regulated industries may require documented evidence that deprecation notifications were sent and acknowledged.
Benefits of a Structured Notification Process
Stakeholders have time to migrate before the hard sunset, reducing emergency incidents.
The notification template creates a standard format that all teams recognize, reducing confusion about what is being deprecated and why.
Migration guides published as part of the notification reduce the per-team migration burden.
Automated API deprecation headers allow callers to detect sunset timelines programmatically without relying on email.
Structured process adds lead time to the deprecation. A version that needs to be removed quickly (security vulnerability) may not be able to follow the full 90-day timeline.
Migration guides require ongoing maintenance. A guide that is accurate at announcement may be stale by the time of the actual sunset.
Notification fatigue is real. If every minor prompt update triggers a deprecation notice, stakeholders learn to ignore them. Reserve the formal process for versions with active callers.

Think of the notification process as a formal contract lifecycle. The announcement is the notice of intent to terminate. The deprecation warning phase is the notice period. The hard sunset is contract termination. As with any contract lifecycle, the other party (the callers) needs formal notice, a clear timeline, and a path forward (the migration guide) before termination takes effect.

attrs.alt: Three-phase sunset timeline: Phase 1 Announcement (Day 0), Phase 2 Deprecation Warning (Day 30-60), Phase 3 Hard Sunset (Day 90) with stakeholder notification matrix

content.text: Notification scope depends on who has a dependency on the version being deprecated. Identify callers before sending notifications:
any team whose code contains the version ID (prompt template name or pinned model ID). Find these by grepping the codebase:
the deprecation may change user-facing behavior. Product owners need to review whether the migration affects features visible to end users and update release notes.
external partners or internal clients calling a public API that exposes the version. Notify via API deprecation headers and developer portal announcement.
SRE or platform on-call needs to know the sunset date so they can plan coverage for the cutover night and handle any incidents.
Every deprecation notification must contain seven components, regardless of the channel it is sent through:
the exact version ID (prompt template name + version, or model ID).
superseded by a better version, cost reduction, security issue, provider end-of-life.
list of known caller teams or services.
link to migration guide, or inline steps if short.
how to revert to the old version if the migration fails (available until hard sunset).
the team or person responsible for the deprecation, reachable for questions.

# [DEPRECATION NOTICE] prompt-summarization-v2 / claude-3-haiku-20240307

**Status:** Deprecated as of 2026-07-18. Hard sunset: 2026-10-01.

## What is being deprecated
- Prompt template: `prompt-summarization-v2` (stored in prompt-registry)
- Pinned model: `claude-3-haiku-20240307`

## Why
Replaced by `prompt-summarization-v3` which uses structured output and reduces
tokens by 30%. The pinned Haiku model is being retired by Anthropic on 2026-09-30.

## Who is affected
- summarisation-service (owns 80% of call volume)
- content-pipeline (calls via REST)
- partner-api consumers using model=claude-3-haiku-20240307

## When
- 2026-07-18: This announcement. New callers must use v3 from today.
- 2026-09-01: Best-effort support only. No new bug fixes.
- 2026-10-01: v2 removed. API returns 410 Gone. Anthropic retires model.

## Migration path
See MIGRATION-v2-to-v3.md in this repository.
Key change: v3 returns JSON, not plain text. Update your output parser.

## Rollback
Set env var PROMPT_VERSION=v2 to revert. Available until 2026-10-01 only.

## Owner
platform-team · #ai-platform on Slack · platform-team@company.com
HTTP API responses should carry standard deprecation headers so clients can detect sunset timelines programmatically, even if they miss email or Slack notifications:
A migration guide published as part of the deprecation notification must contain:

a documented comparison of old vs new behavior. For prompt migrations, include example inputs and the old vs new outputs. For model migrations, note any output format, reasoning, or capability differences.
which callers can migrate with zero code changes (pure config update), which need parser changes, and which need eval before migrating.
exact code changes required. Include before/after code snippets.
how to revert if the migration introduces a regression. Should be a single command or config change.
what callers must verify before considering their migration complete (eval set run, smoke tests, production canary).

# Migration Guide: prompt-summarization-v2 → v3

## Behavior Diff

| Aspect         | v2                        | v3                          |
|----------------|--------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Output format  | Plain text paragraph     | JSON: {summary, keywords}   |
| Token usage    | ~800 output tokens avg   | ~560 output tokens avg      |
| Max input      | 4000 tokens              | 8000 tokens                 |
| Language       | English only             | Multilingual                |

## Who Can Migrate with Zero Code Changes
- None — all callers must update their output parser.

## Step-by-Step Migration

1. Update prompt version config:
```python
# Before
PROMPT_VERSION = "summarization-v2"
# After
PROMPT_VERSION = "summarization-v3"
```

2. Update output parser:
```python
# Before (v2 returns plain text)
summary = llm_response.content

# After (v3 returns JSON)
import json
parsed = json.loads(llm_response.content)
summary = parsed["summary"]
keywords = parsed["keywords"]
```

3. Run eval set against v3:
```bash
pytest evals/test_summarization.py --prompt-version v3
```

## Rollback
```bash
export PROMPT_VERSION=summarization-v2
# Restart service — reverts immediately
```
Rollback available until 2026-10-01 only.

## Owner
platform-team · https://github.com/company/ai-platform/issues/456
notify all stakeholders. Open a tracking GitHub issue. New callers must use the successor version from this day. Existing callers are given until hard sunset to migrate.
full SLA still active. Migration guide published. Weekly reminder Slack posts sent. At Day 60, support level drops to best-effort (security patches only, relaxed response SLA).

traffic cutover to 0% for old version. API returns 410 Gone with a Link header pointing to the successor. Old prompt template archived to read-only storage. Infrastructure decommissioned. Sunset retrospective published.
How to Implement the Notification Process
Identify all callers before announcement. Run a codebase search and API access log query to build the complete caller list. Missing a caller means they get no notice.
Open the tracking GitHub issue with the full notification template as the issue body. Assign it to the platform team. All follow-up communications link to this issue.
Send the email and Slack notification on Day 0. Schedule calendar reminders for Day 30, Day 60, and Day 80 follow-ups.
Deploy the API deprecation headers on Day 0. Monitor header exposure in access logs to verify all callers are receiving them.
Publish the migration guide on Day 0 (at minimum a draft) and finalize by Day 30. Update the guide as migration questions arise.
Track migration progress by monitoring caller traffic to the old version. As teams migrate, their traffic share should decline toward zero.
(which adds structured output and costs 20% less). Three teams use v2: the live chat service (high volume), the ticket routing service (medium volume), and a partner integration (low volume).
Day 0: Announcement email to all three team leads. Tracking issue #789 opened. API deprecation headers deployed. Migration guide published as PR draft.
Day 14: Live chat team migrates in canary (5% traffic). Migration guide updated with their parser changes.
Day 30: Live chat team fully migrated. Ticket routing team begins migration. Weekly Slack reminder sent.
Day 55: Ticket routing team migrated. Partner integration still on v2 — platform team escalates to partner's account manager.
Day 80: Partner integration migrated after escalation. Final Slack reminder: sunset in 10 days.
Day 90: Traffic cutover. v2 archived. GitHub issue #789 closed. Sunset retrospective published.
Announcing without identifying all callers first:
discovering a caller after the announcement means adding them to the process mid-stream. Always identify callers before Day 0.
Sending notifications without a migration guide:

telling teams a version is deprecated without telling them how to migrate creates anxiety without direction. The migration guide should be ready (at minimum as a draft) when the announcement goes out.
Setting the sunset date before identifying migration complexity:
if the migration requires an eval run, a parser rewrite, and a canary deployment, 30 days is not enough. Assess migration complexity for the hardest caller before setting the timeline.
without traffic metrics showing which callers have migrated, the platform team may sunset a version that still has active callers.
Use access logs to identify callers before announcement. Sort by call volume to prioritize outreach to the highest-volume callers first.
Link the tracking GitHub issue from every notification. All discussion, updates, and caller confirmations happen in that one place.
Monitor traffic to the deprecated version weekly during the deprecation window. A caller whose traffic is not declining may need escalation.
Never sunset a version with non-zero traffic without explicit confirmation from all callers that they have migrated.
LangSmith, Weights and Biases Prompts, or a home-built prompt registry backed by Git for version-controlled prompt templates.
GitHub Actions workflow that sends scheduled Slack reminders based on sunset dates in a deprecation registry YAML file.
labeled by version, with a Grafana dashboard showing migration progress.

If a prompt or model version has a security vulnerability (e.g., susceptible to a newly discovered jailbreak pattern), the normal 90-day timeline may be compressed to a 7-14 day emergency sunset. Document the expedited process in the deprecation policy.
Archived deprecated prompts must still be accessible for audit and forensic purposes (e.g., what prompt produced a harmful output on date X). Store in read-only, access-logged storage.

API deprecation headers should be added to all response paths, not just the happy path. Callers using error responses to detect version status will miss sunset timelines if headers are only added on 200 OK responses.
A versioned prompt template or pinned model ID is a stable API contract. Deprecating it requires formal notice, a migration guide, and a clear sunset date.
Identify all callers before announcing. Missing a caller means they get no notice and may face a production incident at the hard sunset.
The three-phase timeline — Announcement (Day 0), Deprecation Warning (Day 30-60), Hard Sunset (Day 90) — gives callers structured lead time with a clear escalation path.
Notification channels include email, Slack, CHANGELOG, API Deprecation headers, and a tracking GitHub issue. Use all channels — do not rely on any single one.
Every notification must include: what, why, when, who is affected, migration path, rollback procedure, and owner contact.
API Deprecation and Sunset headers let callers detect sunset timelines programmatically without relying on email.
Monitor traffic to the deprecated version weekly. Never sunset a version that still has non-zero traffic from an unacknowledged caller.
